Carlo Zaniolo.topics: Deductive database, Datalog, data stream, declarative language
Carlo Zaniolo.key_contributions: Deductive database and Datalog extensions (LDL/LDL++), data stream query language, and recently large-scale distributed Datalog.
Carlo Zaniolo.inclusion_reason: Long-time leader in deductive databases and Datalog. Latest Codd Innovations Award Winner.

Philip A. Bernstein.topics: Concurrency control, distributed transactions, metadata management, model management
Philip A. Bernstein.key_contributions: Textbook of concurrency control and recovery, distributed transaction theory, schema matching and model management.
Philip A. Bernstein.inclusion_reason: A classic authority on concurrency control and metadata management. Early recipient of the Codd Innovations Award.

Wenfei Fan.topics: Data quality, conditional functional dependence, graph pattern matching, big data approximation calculation
Wenfei Fan.key_contributions: Data cleansing theory using conditional functional dependence, graph pattern matching, and bounded evaluation theory for big data.
Wenfei Fan.inclusion_reason: Theoretical authority on data quality and graph data management. He is a member of the Royal Society and also has offices in China.

Divesh Srivastava.topics: Data integration, data quality, truth discovery, stream processing
Divesh Srivastava.key_contributions: Big data integration and truth discovery, data quality, and query processing on streams. Director of VLDB Endowment.
Divesh Srivastava.inclusion_reason: A leading researcher in the data integration and data quality industry (AT&T). The core of the VLDB community.

Tova Milo.topics: Semi-structured data/XML, crowdsourcing data management, data provenance, web data
Tova Milo.key_contributions: Theory of semi-structured data and XML, data management using crowdsourcing, data provenance and business processes.
Tova Milo.inclusion_reason: An authority on database theory and crowdsourcing data management. She is a leading female researcher at PODS and is important for diversity.

Kyuseok Shim.topics: Clustering (CURE), association rules, MapReduce algorithm, cardinality estimation
Kyuseok Shim.key_contributions: CURE/BIRCH Large-scale data processing algorithm using clustering, association rule optimization, and MapReduce.
Kyuseok Shim.inclusion_reason: Korea's leading data mining and database researcher. Co-inventor of classical algorithms such as CURE.

Yizhou Sun.topics: Heterogeneous information network, Metapath (PathSim), graph neural network, knowledge graph
Yizhou Sun.key_contributions: Applied research on heterogeneous information network mining, PathSim metapath similarity, and graph neural networks.
Yizhou Sun.inclusion_reason: Key researcher in heterogeneous information network mining. Active leader and female researcher who won the VLDB Test of Time Award.

Hui Xiong.topics: Spatial data mining, human resources analysis, urban computing, recommendation system
Hui Xiong.key_contributions: Applied research in spatial/spatiotemporal data mining, talent analytics and business intelligence, and urban computing.
Hui Xiong.inclusion_reason: An authority on spatial data mining and applied data mining. Active leader based in Hong Kong/China who contributes to diversity.

Xindong Wu.topics: Rule learning, big data knowledge engineering, web mining, knowledge graph (BigKE)
Xindong Wu.key_contributions: Leading the top 10 algorithm papers in data mining, rule-based learning, and big data knowledge engineering (BigKE). Former editor-in-chief of IEEE TKDE.
Xindong Wu.inclusion_reason: A leading Chinese data mining researcher who led ICDM. Lead author of the ``Top 10 algorithms in data mining'' paper.

Eamonn Keogh.topics: Time series mining, Matrix Profile, dynamic time warping (DTW), anomaly detection/motif discovery
Eamonn Keogh.key_contributions: A leading expert in time series data mining. Established motif/anomaly discovery using DTW lower bound, SAX expression, and Matrix Profile.
Eamonn Keogh.inclusion_reason: World authority on time series mining. He is still a leader in the field with Matrix Profile, etc. Central figure in ICDM.

Jie Tang.topics: Social network mining, academic graph (AMiner), graph representation learning, large-scale language model
Jie Tang.key_contributions: AMiner academic social network analysis platform, social influence analysis, graph representation learning (GLM/CogDL), large-scale language model ChatGLM.
Jie Tang.inclusion_reason: China's leading data mining researcher. AMiner and KDD Test of Time awards, and in recent years, large-scale models have also been leading the way.

Xifeng Yan.topics: Graph pattern mining (gSpan), graph indexing, knowledge graph, dialogue system
Xifeng Yan.key_contributions: gSpan frequent graph mining algorithm, graph indexing and query (gIndex), and recently knowledge graph and interaction systems.
Xifeng Yan.inclusion_reason: Proposed core algorithms for graph mining such as gSpan. A researcher from China who works across databases and mining.

Peter Boncz.topics: Column-oriented DB (MonetDB/VectorWise), vectorization execution, benchmark (LDBC), graph DB processing
Peter Boncz.key_contributions: Established column-oriented/vectorized query execution using MonetDB and VectorWise, led TPC-H/LDBC benchmark, and researched graph DB processing.
Peter Boncz.inclusion_reason: A central figure in modern analytical databases (column-oriented/vectorized execution). VLDB Test of Time Award-winning European systems researcher.

Tamer Özsu.topics: Distributed database, RDF/graph data management, stream processing, data integration
Tamer Özsu.key_contributions: Textbook "Principles of Distributed Database Systems" (field standard), RDF/graph data management, fundamentals of distributed query processing.
Tamer Özsu.inclusion_reason: Author of standard textbook on distributed databases and authority on graph data management. Contributing to geographic diversity with a Canadian base.

Kenneth A. De Jong.topics: Genetic algorithms, unified theory of evolutionary computation, parameter analysis of evolutionary algorithms
Kenneth A. De Jong.key_contributions: One of the founders of evolutionary computation. In his 1975 doctoral thesis, he established the GA benchmark function suite (De Jong test suite). He wrote the textbook "Evolutionary Computation: A Unified Approach" and served as the founding editor-in-chief of the magazine Evolutionary Computation.
Kenneth A. De Jong.inclusion_reason: Founder of the field of evolutionary computing and winner of the IEEE CIS Pioneer Award. Forming the field with GA benchmarks and unified theory.

Kalyanmoy Deb.topics: Evolutionary multi-objective optimization, NSGA-II / NSGA-III, multi-criteria decision making
Kalyanmoy Deb.key_contributions: A central figure in evolutionary multi-objective optimization. NSGA-II is one of the most cited papers in evolutionary computation. It established the EMO field and was widely implemented in commercial optimization software.
Kalyanmoy Deb.inclusion_reason: Representative researcher of EMO and developer of NSGA-II. IEEE CIS Pioneer Award/ACM Fellow. He is from Asia (India) and contributes to diversity.

Carlos A. Coello Coello.topics: Evolutionary multi-objective optimization, biomimetic metaheuristics, constraint processing
Carlos A. Coello Coello.key_contributions: A pioneer in evolutionary multi-objective optimization. Systematizing multi-objective optimization methods using biomimetic stochastic algorithms. Operated the EMOO repository for many years.
Carlos A. Coello Coello.inclusion_reason: EMO principal researcher and IEEE CIS Pioneer Award recipient. He is from Central and South America (Mexico) and contributes to regional diversity.

Marco Dorigo.topics: Ant colony optimization (ACO), swarm intelligence, swarm robotics
Marco Dorigo.key_contributions: Inventor of ant colony optimization (ACO) metaheuristics. Established the field of swarm intelligence and swarm robotics.
Marco Dorigo.inclusion_reason: Founder of swarm intelligence and inventor of ACO. Received the IEEE Frank Rosenblatt Award. A leading researcher in Europe.

Xin Yao.topics: Evolutionary programming, evolutionary neural networks, ensemble learning, coevolution
Xin Yao.key_contributions: A leader in the field of fast evolutionary programming (FEP), evolutionary neural networks, coevolution, and evolutionary ensembles. Former Chairman of IEEE CIS.
Xin Yao.inclusion_reason: A leading researcher in evolutionary computation and the first Chinese recipient of the IEEE Frank Rosenblatt Award. Representing China and contributing to Asia's diversity.

Hisao Ishibuchi.topics: Evolutionary multi-objective optimization, fuzzy rule-based classifier, genetic fuzzy system, performance evaluation index of multi-objective optimization
Hisao Ishibuchi.key_contributions: Fusion of evolutionary multi-objective optimization and fuzzy rule-based classifier design. Widely referenced in performance evaluation and index research for multi-objective optimization.
Hisao Ishibuchi.inclusion_reason: Principal researcher of EMO and fuzzy systems. Born in Japan, currently affiliated with SUSTech, is an IEEE Fellow contributing to diversity in Japan, China and Asia.

Una-May O'Reilly.topics: Genetic programming, evolutionary machine learning, adversarial intelligence, scalable evolutionary computation
Una-May O'Reilly.key_contributions: Early researcher in genetic programming. He leads the ALFA group and researches scalable evolutionary computation and adversarial intelligence. Engaged in editing ACM TELO and Evolutionary Computation magazines.
Una-May O'Reilly.inclusion_reason: Recipient of the EvoStar Award as a leading researcher in the field of genetic programming. Contributing to diversity as a female researcher.

John R. Koza.topics: genetic programming, automatic invention, human competitive outcomes
John R. Koza.key_contributions: Founder of genetic programming (GP). He established the field with his book ``Genetic Programming'' (1992), which demonstrated patent-level automatic inventions (human-competitive results) using GP.
John R. Koza.inclusion_reason: He has classical influence as the founder of genetic programming. A person who built the foundation of the field.

Wolfgang Banzhaf.topics: genetic programming, linear GP, artificial life, evolvability
Wolfgang Banzhaf.key_contributions: Leading researcher in genetic programming. Leading linear GP, artificial chemistry, and evolvability research. Co-founding editor of GPEM magazine.
Wolfgang Banzhaf.inclusion_reason: A classic GP researcher and co-founder of GPEM magazine. Born in Germany, active in Canada and the United States, contributing to European diversity.

Yew-Soon Ong.topics: Memetic computation, evolutionary/transfer optimization, surrogate model-assisted optimization
Yew-Soon Ong.key_contributions: Established the theoretical basis of memetic computation and evolutionary transfer optimization. Founding editor-in-chief of IEEE Trans. on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence.
Yew-Soon Ong.inclusion_reason: A leading researcher in memetic computation and transfer optimization and an IEEE Fellow. Contributing to Asian diversity based in Singapore.

Mengjie Zhang.topics: Genetic programming, evolutionary feature selection, evolutionary scheduling, hyperheuristics
Mengjie Zhang.key_contributions: Leading research on feature selection, image classification, and job shop scheduling using genetic programming. Active in the IEEE CIS Evolutionary Computation Technology Committee.
Mengjie Zhang.inclusion_reason: Principal researcher of GP and IEEE Fellow/FRSNZ. Born in China and based in New Zealand, contributing to the diversity of Australia and Oceania.

Michael Wooldridge.topics: Multi-agent systems, logic of agent cooperation and teamwork, computational complexity, agent-oriented software engineering
Michael Wooldridge.key_contributions: Author of "An Introduction to MultiAgent Systems", a standard textbook on multiagent systems. Established a formal theory of cooperation, teamwork, and communication.
Michael Wooldridge.inclusion_reason: Leading MAS researcher and author of standard textbooks. ACM/AAAI Fellow, IFAAMAS Influential Paper Award winner.

Milind Tambe.topics: Multi-agent teamwork, security games, distributed constraint optimization (DCOP), AI for social good
Milind Tambe.key_contributions: Fundamental contributions in multi-agent teamwork, DCOP, and security games. Established the Stackelberg security game method, which is actually deployed by the U.S. Coast Guard, TSA, etc.
Milind Tambe.inclusion_reason: A leading authority on security games and MAS teamwork, winner of the IFAAMAS Influential Paper Award. Being from India contributes to diversity.

Tuomas Sandholm.topics: Combinatorial auction, automatic mechanism design, electronic market, equilibrium calculation of imperfect information games
Tuomas Sandholm.key_contributions: Fundamental contributions to combinatorial auctions and mechanism design. Advocates automatic mechanism design. Also famous for poker AI (Libratus).
Tuomas Sandholm.inclusion_reason: ACM Fellow and a leading expert on mechanism design and combinatorial auctions. A representative MAS/game theoretic agent. Originally from Finland.

Makoto Yokoo.topics: Distributed constraint satisfaction/optimization (DCOP), mechanism/market design, pseudonym manipulation resistance, game theory
Makoto Yokoo.key_contributions: Fundamental contributions in the formulation of distributed constraint satisfaction problem (DisCSP), algorithms such as ADOPT, false-name-proof mechanism, and matching theory. Former Chairman of IFAAMAS.
Makoto Yokoo.inclusion_reason: A leading researcher in DCOP/mechanism design, Asia's first AAAI Fellow, and former president of IFAAMAS. A researcher representing Japan.

Bo An.topics: Security games, computational game theory, multi-agent reinforcement learning, resource allocation
Bo An.key_contributions: Numerous contributions in security games, computational game theory, and multi-agent reinforcement learning. Application to real-world resource allocation and defense issues.
Bo An.inclusion_reason: Active principal researcher of the security game MARL. Born in China and based in Singapore, he contributes to the diversity of young to mid-career people in Asia.

Kee-Eung Kim.topics: Reinforcement learning, POMDP, inverse reinforcement learning, Bayesian decision making
Kee-Eung Kim.key_contributions: Contributed to POMDP, inverse reinforcement learning, Bayesian reinforcement learning, and multi-agent reinforcement learning. Leading research on sequential decision-making under uncertainty.
Kee-Eung Kim.inclusion_reason: Korea's leading researcher in RL/decision making. Contributing to regional diversity in Korea.

Edmund H. Durfee.topics: Distributed artificial intelligence, multi-agent cooperation, distributed planning, meta-level control
Edmund H. Durfee.key_contributions: A pioneer in distributed artificial intelligence and multi-agent collaboration. We laid the foundation for distributed planning, cooperative control, and distributed constraint satisfaction.
Edmund H. Durfee.inclusion_reason: A classic pioneer of distributed AI/MAS collaboration and AAAI Fellow. A person who built the foundation of the field.

Vincent Conitzer.topics: Computational social selection, automatic mechanism design, computation of game-theoretic solution concepts, AI and ethics
Vincent Conitzer.key_contributions: Fundamental contributions in computational social selection, automatic mechanism design (in collaboration with Sandholm), and the complexity of game-theoretic equilibrium calculations. He also leads AI collaboration and ethics research.
Vincent Conitzer.inclusion_reason: He is an active leading researcher in computational social selection and mechanism design and received the IJCAI Computers and Thought Award. Originally from the Netherlands.

Frank van Harmelen.topics: Semantic Web, Ontology Language (OWL), RDF Repository (Sesame), Hybrid AI
Frank van Harmelen.key_contributions: Co-designer of the W3C ontology OWL. He was involved in the design of the widely used RDF repository Sesame. Co-authored the first Semantic Web textbook, "A Semantic Web Primer."
Frank van Harmelen.inclusion_reason: A central figure in the early days of the Semantic Web and co-designer of OWL. A leading researcher in the Netherlands.

Steffen Staab.topics: Knowledge graph, ontology engineering, web science, intelligent user interface
Steffen Staab.key_contributions: Principal researcher in ontology engineering and knowledge graphs. Founded the WeST Research Institute and led knowledge network research based on conceptual models and ontologies.
Steffen Staab.inclusion_reason: Representative researcher of Semantic Web and knowledge graphs. Contributing to European diversity based in Germany and the UK.

Pascal Hitzler.topics: Semantic Web, Neurosymbolic integration, ontology alignment, Linked Open Data
Pascal Hitzler.key_contributions: Leading research on Semantic Web, neurosymbolic AI, and ontology alignment. Founding editor-in-chief of Semantic Web Journal.
Pascal Hitzler.inclusion_reason: A representative researcher in Semantic Web/Neurosymbolic and winner of the SWSA Ten-Year Award. Born in Germany and based in the US.

Enrico Motta.topics: Knowledge technology, ontology engineering, academic knowledge graph, semantic web
Enrico Motta.key_contributions: Principal researcher in knowledge technology/ontology engineering. Leading research on academic knowledge graphs and knowledge systems. He has played a central role in major conferences such as ISWC for many years.
Enrico Motta.inclusion_reason: Leading researcher in Semantic Web and knowledge technology. Born in Italy and based in the UK, contributing to European diversity.

Huajun Chen.topics: Knowledge graph, knowledge graph embedding, knowledge-enhanced machine learning, OpenKG
Huajun Chen.key_contributions: Leading researcher in knowledge graph and semantic technology. Chief advisor of Alibaba's e-commerce knowledge graph and one of the founders of OpenKG (China's largest open knowledge graph association).
Huajun Chen.inclusion_reason: A leading researcher in knowledge graph research in China and the founder of OpenKG. Contributing to diversity in Asia (China).

Lora Aroyo.topics: Crowdsourcing correct answer data construction (CrowdTruth), user modeling, digital humanities, responsible AI data
Lora Aroyo.key_contributions: Developed the CrowdTruth crowdsourcing method with Chris Welty and established correct answer data construction that takes ambiguity into account. Bridging the Semantic Web and human computation.
Lora Aroyo.inclusion_reason: A researcher who bridges the Semantic Web and crowdsourcing (HCOMP) and developer of CrowdTruth. A female researcher from Bulgaria who contributes to diversity.

Elena Simperl.topics: Knowledge graph, crowdsourcing/citizen science, human data interaction, open data
Elena Simperl.key_contributions: Leading research on human-machine collectives that combines knowledge graphs and crowdsourcing. Leader in citizen science, knowledge communities, and data policy.
Elena Simperl.inclusion_reason: A researcher who bridges knowledge graphs and crowdsourcing. A female researcher from Romania who contributes to regional and gender diversity.

Jérôme Euzenat.topics: Ontology matching/alignment, knowledge representation, semantic interoperability, semantic web
Jérôme Euzenat.key_contributions: Established ontology matching field. He wrote the standard textbook Ontology Matching (co-authored with Shvaiko) and led the OAEI evaluation campaign for many years.
Jérôme Euzenat.inclusion_reason: A leading expert on ontology matching and author of standard textbooks. A leading French researcher who contributes to European diversity.

Jeff Z. Pan.topics: Knowledge graph, descriptive logical reasoning, ontology, knowledge graph enhanced LLM
Jeff Z. Pan.key_contributions: Descriptive logical reasoning and scalable knowledge graph processing.In recent years, he has been leading research on LLM enhancement using knowledge graphs.
Jeff Z. Pan.inclusion_reason: Active major researcher in descriptive logic and knowledge graphs. Born in China and based in the UK, he contributes to the diversity of Asian and European bases.

Joseph A. Konstan.topics: Collaborative filtering, GroupLens / MovieLens, recommended user experience, online participation promotion
Joseph A. Konstan.key_contributions: Central figure in the GroupLens collaborative filtering project and MovieLens dataset. Chair of the 1st ACM RecSys Conference.
Joseph A. Konstan.inclusion_reason: A central figure in the early days of recommendation systems/collaborative filtering and won the ACM Software System Award. Involved in the creation of the RecSys conference.

John T. Riedl.topics: Collaborative filtering, GroupLens, recommendation algorithm, shilling attack
John T. Riedl.key_contributions: Co-founded the GroupLens project in 1992 and established personalized recommendations through automatic collaborative filtering. Co-founded recommendation system company Net Perceptions.
John T. Riedl.inclusion_reason: He is one of the founders of collaborative filtering/recommendation systems and won the ACM Software System Award. laid the classical foundations of the field.

Francesco Ricci.topics: Recommendation system, context-aware recommendation, tourism/travel recommendation, Recommender Systems Handbook
Francesco Ricci.key_contributions: Co-editor of the Recommender Systems Handbook, which established the standard reference in the field. Leading the way in context-aware tourism recommendations. Former Chair of ACM RecSys Steering Committee.
Francesco Ricci.inclusion_reason: Representative researcher and handbook editor of recommendation systems, and former chair of the RecSys Steering Committee. A leading researcher in Italy.

Dietmar Jannach.topics: Session-based recommendation, conversational recommendation, recommendation evaluation methodology, practical application of recommendation
Dietmar Jannach.key_contributions: Principal researcher on session-based recommendation/conversational recommendation. He is famous for his critical research on recommendation system evaluation methodologies and reproducibility. Co-author of the textbook "Recommender Systems: An Introduction".
Dietmar Jannach.inclusion_reason: Active principal researcher on recommendation systems and member of the RecSys steering committee. Contributing to European diversity based in Austria.

Lior Rokach.topics: Recommender systems, ensemble learning, decision trees, Recommender Systems Handbook
Lior Rokach.key_contributions: Co-editor of the Recommender Systems Handbook. Conducts widely referenced research in recommender systems, ensemble learning, and data mining.
Lior Rokach.inclusion_reason: Editor and principal researcher of the Recommendation System Handbook. Contributing to the diversity of the Middle East region based in Israel.

Bracha Shapira.topics: Information filtering, recommendation system, cross-domain recommendation, Recommender Systems Handbook
Bracha Shapira.key_contributions: Co-editor of the Recommender Systems Handbook. Principal researcher of information filtering and recommendation systems.
Bracha Shapira.inclusion_reason: Editor and principal researcher of the Recommendation System Handbook. Contributing to regional and gender diversity as a female researcher based in Israel.

Robin Burke.topics: Hybrid recommendation, fairness of recommendation, multi-stakeholder recommendation, recommendation algorithm
Robin Burke.key_contributions: Established classification of hybrid recommendation systems (2002). Leading research on recommendation fairness and multi-stakeholder recommendation.
Robin Burke.inclusion_reason: Representative researcher on hybrid recommendations and recommendation fairness. Influential in both classical (hybrid nomination) and current (fairness) aspects.

Xiangnan He.topics: Neural Collaborative Filtering (NCF), Graph Neural Recommendation (NGCF/LightGCN), Implicit Feedback, Causal Recommendation
Xiangnan He.key_contributions: Proposes influential methods for deep/graph-based recommendation such as Neural Collaborative Filtering (NCF), NGCF, and LightGCN. Leading recommendation research on implicit feedback.
Xiangnan He.inclusion_reason: Active top researcher (NCF/LightGCN) with deep/graph-based recommendations. Representing China and contributing to the diversity of Asia and the young generation.

Tat-Seng Chua.topics: Neural recommendation, multimodal analysis, conversational search/recommendation, causal inference
Tat-Seng Chua.key_contributions: Leading researcher in multimodal data analysis and neural recommendation. Co-author of NCF. Driving robust and reliable AI through conversational search/recommendation and causal inference.
Tat-Seng Chua.inclusion_reason: ACM Fellow and a leading researcher in multimodal recommendation and information retrieval. Contributing to diversity in Asia with our Singapore base.

Paolo Cremonesi.topics: Recommendation algorithm, evaluation of Top-N recommendation, reproducibility, performance comparison of recommendation
Paolo Cremonesi.key_contributions: An influential study that established the evaluation methodology for Top-N recommendation tasks (RecSys 2010). Leading research on reproducibility and performance comparison of recommendation algorithms.
Paolo Cremonesi.inclusion_reason: A leading researcher on recommendation evaluation methodology and a debater on reproducibility research. Contributing to European diversity with our base in Italy.

Luis von Ahn.topics: Human computation (crowdsourcing), CAPTCHA / reCAPTCHA, GWAP (game-based labor), Duolingo
Luis von Ahn.key_contributions: Founder of the concept of human computation (crowdsourcing). Invented CAPTCHA, reCAPTCHA, and ESP games (GWAP), and co-founded Duolingo.
Luis von Ahn.inclusion_reason: Founder of Human Computation/Crowdsourcing and MacArthur Fellow. Originally from Guatemala, she contributes to regional diversity.

Jennifer Wortman Vaughan.topics: Crowdsourcing, Prediction markets, Human-AI interaction, Mathematical foundations of social computing
Jennifer Wortman Vaughan.key_contributions: Leading mathematical basic research in crowdsourcing, prediction markets, and social calculations. He has written a comprehensive survey of crowdsourcing for machine learning, and has recently researched human-AI interaction.
Jennifer Wortman Vaughan.inclusion_reason: A key researcher who bridges crowdsourcing/human computation and ML. Contributing to diversity as a female researcher.

Edith Law.topics: Human computation system design, human-machine cooperation, citizen science, crowdsourcing
Edith Law.key_contributions: Researching the design of human computation systems that leverage joint efforts between humans and machines. Co-organized the HCOMP workshop/conference and co-authored the book Human Computation.
Edith Law.inclusion_reason: Leading researcher in the field of human computation, organizer of HCOMP, and co-author of the book. A female researcher based in Canada who contributes to regional and gender diversity.

Aniket Kittur.topics: Crowdsourcing of complex and creative tasks, future of crowdwork, knowledge integration, collaborative information synthesis
Aniket Kittur.key_contributions: Pioneering contribution to the application of crowdsourcing to complex and creative tasks. Leading "The Future of Crowd Work". He leads the Knowledge Acceleration Center.
Aniket Kittur.inclusion_reason: Leading researcher in crowdsourcing/human computation and CHI Academy. Pioneering crowdsourcing for complex tasks.

Matthew Lease.topics: Crowdsourcing, human computation, information search evaluation, human-centered AI
Matthew Lease.key_contributions: Research to bridge crowdsourcing and information retrieval. Crowdsourcing for search evaluation, quality control, and co-author of "The Future of Crowd Work."
Matthew Lease.inclusion_reason: A key researcher who bridges crowdsourcing and information retrieval, and one of the central figures at HCOMP.

Markus Zanker.topics: Knowledge-based recommendation, constraint-based recommendation, recommendation explanation, e-commerce recommendation
Markus Zanker.key_contributions: Principal researcher of knowledge-based/constraint-based recommendation. Co-author of the textbook "Recommender Systems: An Introduction". Research on recommendation explanations and e-commerce applications. Member of RecSys Steering Committee.
Markus Zanker.inclusion_reason: Principal researcher of Knowledge Base Recommendation, textbook co-author, and member of RecSys Steering Committee. Contributing to European diversity with our Austria/Italy base.

Yi Mei.topics: Genetic programming, evolutionary scheduling, hyperheuristics, combinatorial optimization
Yi Mei.key_contributions: Leading researcher in dynamic job shop scheduling using genetic programming, vehicle route problems, and hyperheuristics.
Yi Mei.inclusion_reason: Active major researcher in GP/evolutionary scheduling. Born in China and based in NZ, he contributes to the young generation and the diversity of Oceania.

Riccardo Poli.topics: Genetic programming theory, PSO theory, GP schema theory, brain computer interface
Riccardo Poli.key_contributions: Principal researcher of genetic programming theory (schema theory) and particle swarm optimization (PSO) theory. Co-author of "A Field Guide to Genetic Programming".
Riccardo Poli.inclusion_reason: Representative researcher of GP theory and PSO theory and co-author of Field Guide. Born in Italy and based in the UK, contributing to European diversity.

Ben Shneiderman.topics: Direct operation interface, information visualization, treemap, human-centered AI, usability
Ben Shneiderman.key_contributions: Proposed the concept of direct operation interface (1981). Founded HCIL. Established the fundamentals of treemaps, dynamic queries, and information visualization. In recent years, he has proposed a two-dimensional HCAI framework that enhances automation while maintaining human control with ``Human-Centered AI.''
Ben Shneiderman.inclusion_reason: A classical giant of HCI and a modern leader in the theoretical framework of human-centered AI. Most important as a connection point between AI and HCI.

Eric Horvitz.topics: Human-AI collaboration, decision support, mixed initiative interface, AI ethics, probabilistic reasoning
Eric Horvitz.key_contributions: Developed principles and mechanisms for machine learning and inference using probability and utility, mixed-initiative interfaces, and human-AI complementarity. Leads AI Ethics (Aether Committee).
Eric Horvitz.inclusion_reason: Award-winning industry leader at the intersection of AI and HCI. Mixed-driven UI is a fundamental concept of human-centered AI.

Aniket (Niki) Kittur.topics: Crowdsourcing, collective intelligence, AI augmented cognition, knowledge synthesis, complex crowdwork
Aniket (Niki) Kittur.key_contributions: Build a scientific foundation for large-scale social production systems (Wikipedia, etc.). Pioneering the application of crowdsourcing to complex and creative tasks. Researching AI augmented cognition that combines human and machine intelligence.
Aniket (Niki) Kittur.inclusion_reason: A central figure in crowdsourcing and collective intelligence. Joined CHI Academy in 2022.

Jeffrey P. Bigham.topics: Accessibility, Real-time crowdsourcing, Responsible use of generative AI, Visual impairment support
Jeffrey P. Bigham.key_contributions: Developed real-time crowdsourcing systems such as VizWiz and applied them to support people with disabilities. In recent years, he has been building processes and technologies for the responsible use of generative AI.
Jeffrey P. Bigham.inclusion_reason: A leading expert on accessibility x crowdsourcing x AI. Joined CHI Academy in 2026. Both academia and industry.

Meredith Ringel Morris.topics: Human-AI interaction, gesture manipulation, collaborative web search, accessibility, human-centered AI
Meredith Ringel Morris.key_contributions: Fundamental contributions in collaborative web search, gesture interaction, and accessible technologies. Founded the Ability Research Group at MSR. Leads human-AI interaction research at Google DeepMind.
Meredith Ringel Morris.inclusion_reason: Core researcher of human-AI interaction. An active industry leader who leads HCI research in generative AI.

Saleema Amershi.topics: Human-AI interaction design guidelines, interactive machine learning, Responsible AI, HAX Toolkit
Saleema Amershi.key_contributions: As the lead author of "Guidelines for Human-AI Interaction" (CHI 2019), he has integrated over 20 years of knowledge into 18 design guidelines. Promoting Interactive ML and HAX Toolkit.
Saleema Amershi.inclusion_reason: A central figure who created standards for human-AI interaction design. Representative researcher of interactive ML.

Krzysztof Z. Gajos.topics: Adaptive user interface, intelligent interactive system, interactive ML, large-scale behavioral experiment, accessibility
Krzysztof Z. Gajos.key_contributions: Established the design space and evaluation of automatic personalized UI generation and adaptive UI using SUPPLE. Developed large-scale behavioral research methods at LabintheWild.
Krzysztof Z. Gajos.inclusion_reason: Representative researcher of intelligent/adaptive UI and interactive ML. He is from Europe and represents academia.

Elena L. Glassman.topics: Human-AI interaction, program synthesis UI, large-scale corpus understanding support, ML interpretability interface
Elena L. Glassman.key_contributions: Designed an easy-to-use interactive system that utilizes program synthesis and an interface to understand the structure of large-scale code and data sets. Applying learning science theory (Variation Theory) to UI design.
Elena L. Glassman.inclusion_reason: Up-and-coming artist in human-AI interaction and program synthesis UI. Representative of current young people.

Takeo Igarashi.topics: Sketch-based modeling (Teddy), interactive CG, shape deformation, user interface, robot interaction
Takeo Igarashi.key_contributions: Developed multi-touch shape deformation method for sketch-based 3D modeling system "Teddy". Served as program chair for UIST 2013/2016 and CHI 2021.
Takeo Igarashi.inclusion_reason: Japan's leading HCI/UIST researcher. The creator of the classic interactive interface "Teddy".

Xiaojuan Ma.topics: Mobile interaction, eye tracking, human-AI collaboration, crowdsourcing, social computing
Xiaojuan Ma.key_contributions: Research on mobile interaction/mobile intelligence, eye tracking, and human-AI collaboration. Received CHI 2024 Best Paper and CHI 2025 Best Paper Honorable Mention.
Xiaojuan Ma.inclusion_reason: Active HCI researcher representing Asia. Active in human-AI collaboration and mobile interaction.

Kun-Pyo Lee.topics: User-centered design, interaction design, UX design, design methodology
Kun-Pyo Lee.key_contributions: Introduced the concept of UX to Korea for the first time. Establishment of user-centered design and interaction design. Founding member and honorary chairman of IASDR. He also served as the director of LG Electronics' design center.
Kun-Pyo Lee.inclusion_reason: A leading figure in Korean HCI/interaction design. Asian diversity and design x industry perspective.

Ed Huai-Hsin Chi.topics: Information visualization, recommendation system, neural recommendation, social computation, large-scale language model, dialogue system
Ed Huai-Hsin Chi.key_contributions: Fundamental contributions to information visualization, social computation, and recommendation systems. LaMDA (Bard/Gemini), led the research and development of neural recommendation and dialogue systems.
Ed Huai-Hsin Chi.inclusion_reason: Industry leader bridging HCI and machine learning/recommendation systems. Born in Taiwan and contributes to diversity.

James A. Landay.topics: Sketch-based UI design tool, ubiquitous computing, behavior change technology, human-centered AI

W. Bruce Croft.inclusion_reason: Salton Award Winner. He is the father of language model search and has had a tremendous influence on human resource development in the IR field through CIIR.

Susan Dumais.topics: Latent Semantic Index (LSI), Web Search, Personalized Search, Temporal Dynamics, HCIR
Susan Dumais.key_contributions: One of the inventors of latent semantic indexing (LSI). Great contribution to the fusion of information retrieval and HCI, personalization, and temporal retrieval.
Susan Dumais.inclusion_reason: Winner of the Salton Award and member of the National Academy of Engineering. A female researcher who is the inventor of LSI and leads HCIR.

Hang Li.topics: Ranking learning, deep learning for semantic matching, question answering, dialogue, language models
Hang Li.key_contributions: Contributed to the systemization of ranking learning (textbook writing) and deep semantic matching models for search and NLP. Founded and led ByteDance AI Lab.
Hang Li.inclusion_reason: A Chinese industry leader who bridges ranking learning and neural IR. Leads AI research at ByteDance.

Maarten de Rijke.topics: Online/offline ranking learning, neural search, conversational search, click model, recommendation
Maarten de Rijke.key_contributions: Widely contributed to online unbiased ranking learning, neural search, and conversational search. Leading the community with NN4IR tutorials, etc.
Maarten de Rijke.inclusion_reason: A central figure in European IR research. We have cultivated a large number of human resources as active leaders in online ranking learning and neural search.

Tetsuya Sakai.topics: Search evaluation index, graded suitability, NTCIR test collections, statistical test, evaluation methodology
Tetsuya Sakai.key_contributions: Fundamental contributions to information retrieval evaluation methodologies (graded suitability indicators, statistical tests, robustness to imperfect suitability determinations). Leads NTCIR.
Tetsuya Sakai.inclusion_reason: World authority on search evaluation methodology. A Japanese researcher who leads IR evaluation research in Asia through NTCIR.

Jamie Callan.topics: Distributed information retrieval, neural sparse/dense retrieval, ClueWeb collections, search augmented generation (RAG)
Jamie Callan.key_contributions: Contributes to distributed information retrieval and neural sparse/dense retrieval. ClueWeb builds and provides large-scale web collections to the research community.
Jamie Callan.inclusion_reason: A CMU professor who contributes to search research infrastructure such as TREC/ClueWeb and is a leader in modern neural search research.

Charles L. A. Clarke.topics: Search evaluation, diversity ranking (novelty/diversity), TREC Web Track, question answer
Charles L. A. Clarke.key_contributions: Leading search result diversity/novelty evaluation (αNDCG, etc.) and TREC Web Track. Contributed to MultiText search system and QA research.
Charles L. A. Clarke.inclusion_reason: Contribution to search evaluation (especially diversity) and TREC infrastructure. Our Canadian base also contributes to regional diversity.

Jimmy Lin.topics: Text ranking with BERT, dense search/sparse search, Anserini/Pyserini toolkit, reproducibility, RAG
Jimmy Lin.key_contributions: Systematization of text ranking using Transformer/BERT (``Pretrained Transformers for Text Ranking''). Promoting neural search research that can be reproduced with Anserini/Pyserini.
Jimmy Lin.inclusion_reason: Active leader in neural search (BERT re-ranking/dense search) and its reproducibility research.

Nick Craswell.topics: Neural ranking, MS MARCO dataset, TREC Deep Learning Track, Web ranking evaluation
Nick Craswell.key_contributions: Leads the large-scale neural search training data MS MARCO and TREC Deep Learning Track and builds an evaluation platform for deep search research.
Nick Craswell.inclusion_reason: Leader of the modern neural search evaluation infrastructure called MS MARCO/TREC DL. An industrial researcher from Australia.

Bhaskar Mitra.topics: Neural Ranking Model (Duet), MS MARCO, TREC Deep Learning Track, Fairness/Responsible IR
Bhaskar Mitra.key_contributions: Contributed to the systemization of neural search models (Duet, etc.) and neural IR. He also co-led the MS MARCO/TREC DL Track and promoted equity research.
Bhaskar Mitra.inclusion_reason: An active researcher who was responsible for popularizing neural IR (NN4IR tutorial survey). Contributing to the diversity of Indian descent.

Emine Yilmaz.topics: Search evaluation index, evaluation scale for ranking learning, user model, conversational search, LLM evaluation
Emine Yilmaz.key_contributions: Contributed to the theory of search evaluation metrics (expected reverse rank ERR, etc.) and evaluation scales for ranking learning. Also researches conversational search and LLM evaluation.
Emine Yilmaz.inclusion_reason: A female leader who connects the theories of search evaluation and ranking learning. Originally from Türkiye, she contributes to regional and gender diversity.

Yiqun Liu.topics: Web search, user behavior modeling, search evaluation, click model, RAG
Yiqun Liu.key_contributions: Contributed to web search user behavior modeling and search evaluation. Leads the international community as FnTIR co-editor-in-chief and SIGIR-AP steering committee chair.
Yiqun Liu.inclusion_reason: A central figure in Chinese IR research. He is an active leader in user behavior and search evaluation and is deeply involved in SIGIR management.

Marc Najork.topics: Link-based ranking, web crawling, ranking learning, neural search, LLM search
Marc Najork.key_contributions: Contributes to link-based ranking, web crawling, and ranking learning. From MSR to Google, he consistently led web search research.
Marc Najork.inclusion_reason: A member of SIGIR Academy and a long-time leader in web search research. Active in industry (MSR/Google).

Hsin-Min Wang.topics: Speech information retrieval, speech recognition, speaker verification, music information processing
Hsin-Min Wang.key_contributions: Widely contributed to voice information retrieval, voice recognition, and music information processing. Leads speech and acoustic processing research in Taiwan and participates in international standardization.
Hsin-Min Wang.inclusion_reason: An important Taiwanese researcher who deals with the boundaries between voice, search, and music. Contributing to regional diversity in the voice information search field.

Chin-Hui Lee.topics: Principles and practices of automatic speech/speaker recognition, adaptive learning, discriminative learning, utterance verification, speech enhancement
Chin-Hui Lee.key_contributions: Pioneering and fundamental contributions to the principles and practices of automatic speech and speaker recognition. Achieving fundamental innovations in adaptive learning, discriminative learning, and utterance verification. Leads dialogue systems research at Bell Labs.
Chin-Hui Lee.inclusion_reason: A classical master who won the ISCA Medal (2012). Originally from Taiwan, he built the foundations of speech and speaker recognition through Bell Labs/Georgia Tech.

Avi Wigderson.topics: Computational complexity theory, randomness and pseudorandom numbers, derandomization, zero-knowledge proofs, combinatorics
Avi Wigderson.key_contributions: Redefining the role of randomness in calculations. His fundamental research on Nisan-Wigderson's pseudo-random number generator, the trade-off between hardness and randomness (results in the direction of P=BPP), and zero-knowledge proofs had a great impact on computational complexity.
Avi Wigderson.inclusion_reason: The only person to have won the Turing Award, Abel Award, and Nevanlinna Award. A master who laid the modern foundations of computational complexity.

Andrew Chi-Chih Yao.topics: Computational complexity, communication complexity, pseudorandom number generation, cryptography, quantum computing
Andrew Chi-Chih Yao.key_contributions: Established the theory of communication complexity and pioneered Yao's minimax principle, the computational basis of pseudorandom number generation, quantum circuit complexity, etc. Established a world-class TCS research center (IIIS) in China.
Andrew Chi-Chih Yao.inclusion_reason: Founder of Communication Complexity and Turing Prize Winner. A central figure in building the TCS community in Asia (China).

Robert E. Tarjan.topics: Graph algorithms, data structures, balanced search trees, network flows, amortized analysis
Robert E. Tarjan.key_contributions: Constructed many basic algorithms and data structures, such as depth-first search-based graph algorithms (strongly connected components and planarity determination), splay trees, Fibonacci heaps, and union-find analysis.
Robert E. Tarjan.inclusion_reason: A master who laid the foundations of modern algorithm theory and data structures. Winner of the inaugural Nevanlinna Prize and Turing Prize.

Leslie Valiant.topics: Computational learning theory (PAC learning), computational complexity, #P completeness, parallel computing model, theory of evolvability
Leslie Valiant.key_contributions: He proposed the PAC learning model and laid the foundation for machine learning theory. He has made fundamental contributions in many fields, including permanent #P completeness, BSP parallel calculation models, and holographic algorithms.
Leslie Valiant.inclusion_reason: Opened the boundary between learning theory and TCS. A master who created the theoretical foundation of AI learning theory (PAC).

Shafi Goldwasser.topics: Cryptography, probabilistic cryptography, zero-knowledge proofs, interactive proofs, computational complexity, property testing
Shafi Goldwasser.key_contributions: Established the basis of interactive proofs leading to probabilistic cryptography, semantic security, zero-knowledge interactive proofs, and probabilistic verifiable proofs (PCP), and made modern cryptography a science.
Shafi Goldwasser.inclusion_reason: One of the founders of modern cryptography, which is backed by computational complexity. Turing Award winner, Israeli native, and contributor to diversity.

Silvio Micali.topics: Cryptography, zero-knowledge proofs, secure multiparty computation, pseudorandom numbers, blockchain theory
Silvio Micali.key_contributions: Together we will build the theoretical foundations of modern cryptography, including probabilistic cryptography, zero-knowledge interactive proofs, verifiable random functions, and secure computational protocols.
Silvio Micali.inclusion_reason: Turing Prize winner who laid the foundations of cryptographic theory. His theory of proof and randomness also influenced complexity theory.

Christos H. Papadimitriou.topics: Computational complexity, algorithmic game theory, optimization, fusion of computation with economics and biology
Christos H. Papadimitriou.key_contributions: We defined the complexity class PPAD and created a framework for studying the difficulty of Nash equilibrium calculations. Leading the way in organizing combinatorial optimization/algorithms into textbooks and integrating calculations with other fields (economy, biology, brain).
Christos H. Papadimitriou.inclusion_reason: A leading expert in bridging computational complexity and algorithmic game theory. Recipient of Knuth Prize and Gödel Prize.

Madhu Sudan.topics: Error correcting codes, probabilistic verifiable proofs (PCP), list decoding, computational complexity, difficulty of approximation
Madhu Sudan.key_contributions: He had a great impact on the proof of the PCP theorem and its application to approximation difficulties, the list decoding algorithm for Reed-Solomon codes, and the basis of property testing.
Madhu Sudan.inclusion_reason: A leading expert on PCP theory and list decoding. Born in India, won the Nevanlinna Award and contributed to diversity.

Daniel A. Spielman.topics: Spectral graph theory, fast solution of linear equations, smoothing analysis, coding theory, optimization
Daniel A. Spielman.key_contributions: Significant impact on calculation and mathematics through smoothed analysis of the simplex method, quasi-linear time solver for Laplacian linear systems, spectral sparsification, and solving the Kadison-Singer problem.
Daniel A. Spielman.inclusion_reason: Founder of spectral algorithms and smoothing analysis. Recipient of Nevanlinna Award and Knuth Award.

Subhash Khot.topics: Computational complexity, difficulty of approximation, Unique Games Conjecture, probabilistic verifiable proof, discrete analysis
Subhash Khot.key_contributions: He proposed the Unique Games Conjecture, which enabled a unified explanation of the difficulty of approximation of many optimization problems. It produced one of the most studied conjectures in theoretical computer science.
Subhash Khot.inclusion_reason: Proponent of UGC, which is central to approximation difficulty research. Originally from India, she contributed to diversity by winning the Nevanlinna Award.

Constantinos Daskalakis.topics: Algorithmic game theory, Nash equilibrium complexity, auction design, machine learning theory, learning probability distributions
Constantinos Daskalakis.key_contributions: Prove that Nash equilibrium calculation is PPAD complete. Exploring the boundaries between TCS, economics, and machine learning, including analysis of the complexity of multi-item auctions, distribution learning, and EM algorithms.
Constantinos Daskalakis.inclusion_reason: A leading expert on the boundary between algorithmic game theory and learning theory. A top active researcher who received the Nevanlinna Prize.

Mark Braverman.topics: Information complexity, communication complexity, computability and analysis, algorithmic game theory, machine learning theory
Mark Braverman.key_contributions: Developed a theory of information complexity and established its relationship with communication complexity and interactive information theory. We have laid the foundation for applications in streaming, distributed computing, and information security.
Mark Braverman.inclusion_reason: Founder of information complexity theory. Current top winner of the first Abacus Medal.

Peter W. Shor.topics: Quantum computing, quantum algorithms, quantum error correction, quantum information theory, combinatorial optimization
Peter W. Shor.key_contributions: Discovered Shor's algorithm for solving prime factorization of integers and discrete logarithms in polynomial time. He also proposed quantum error-correcting codes, laying the theoretical foundation for the feasibility of quantum computing.
Peter W. Shor.inclusion_reason: Discoverer of the Shor algorithm, which pioneered the field of quantum computing. Nevanlinna Award-winning master.

Umesh Vazirani.topics: Quantum computing, quantum complexity, quantum verification, algorithms, computational complexity
Umesh Vazirani.key_contributions: Co-founded quantum complexity theory. Build the theoretical foundations of the Bernstein-Vazirani algorithm, quantum Fourier transform, and quantum device verification (quantum supremacy verification, etc.).
Umesh Vazirani.inclusion_reason: One of the founders of quantum complexity theory. An active stalwart from India who contributes to diversity.

Cynthia Dwork.topics: Differential privacy, cryptography, distributed computing, algorithmic fairness, computational complexity
Cynthia Dwork.key_contributions: Introducing and developing differential privacy to make data privacy a science. He also contributed to the foundations of distributed computing, fault-tolerant consensus, the concept of proof-of-work, and algorithmic fairness.
Cynthia Dwork.inclusion_reason: Founder of differential privacy and foundation of AI privacy theory. Knuth Prize winner, female researcher who contributes to diversity.

Johan Håstad.topics: Computational complexity, difficulty of approximation, PCP, circuit complexity, pseudorandom numbers
Johan Håstad.key_contributions: We laid the foundations for computational complexity with the switching lemma of constant depth circuits, the optimal approximability results (tight inapproximability such as MAX-3SAT), and the configuration of pseudorandom number generators.
Johan Håstad.inclusion_reason: A leading expert who has achieved optimal results for circuit lower bounds and approximation difficulties. Winner of Knuth Prize and Gödel Prize, representing Europe (Nordic Europe).

Noga Alon.topics: Combinatorics, graph theory, stochastic methods, pseudorandom numbers, streaming algorithms, computational complexity
Noga Alon.key_contributions: Applying probabilistic and algebraic methods to TCS. Fundamental contributions in Alon-Matias-Szegedy's streaming frequency moment estimation, color-coding algorithm, graph spectrum and expanded graph theory.
Noga Alon.inclusion_reason: A master at the boundary between combinatorics and algorithms. Laid the foundation for streaming algorithms. Representing Israel.

Oded Goldreich.topics: Cryptography theory, computational complexity, pseudorandom numbers, property testing, zero-knowledge proofs
Oded Goldreich.key_contributions: Systematizes the theoretical foundations of modern cryptography (textbook on basics and applications of cryptography). Connecting computational complexity and cryptography through basic research on pseudo-random numbers, zero-knowledge, secure computation, and property testing.
Oded Goldreich.inclusion_reason: An important figure who systematized cryptography theory and computational complexity. Knuth Prize winner, representing Israel.

Manuel Blum.topics: Computational complexity, cryptography, CAPTCHA, program checking, computational theory
Manuel Blum.key_contributions: He laid the foundations for computational complexity axioms (Blum's axioms), cryptographic pseudorandom numbers (Blum-Blum-Shub), coin tossing protocols, and program inspection/CAPTCHA.
Manuel Blum.inclusion_reason: Turing Prize winner who laid the foundations of computational complexity and cryptography. Being from South America (Venezuela) also contributes to diversity.

Richard M. Karp.topics: NP completeness, combinatorial optimization, randomization algorithm, computational biology, online algorithm
Richard M. Karp.key_contributions: Presented 21 NP-complete problems and established a framework for computational difficulty research. Contributed to numerous algorithms such as Edmonds-Karp, Hopcroft-Karp, and Rabin-Karp, and to the foundations of randomization and parallel computing.
Richard M. Karp.inclusion_reason: Founder of NP completeness theory and master of algorithm theory. Winner of the Turing Award.

László Lovász.topics: Combinatorial optimization, graph theory, semidefinite programming, LLL lattice basis reduction, computational complexity
László Lovász.key_contributions: He laid the foundations of combinatorial optimization and TCS, including Lovász local lemma, LLL lattice basis reduction algorithm (important for cryptanalysis and integer programming), semidefinite programming (theta function), and graph limit theory.
László Lovász.inclusion_reason: A master of the boundaries of combinatorial optimization and computation. Recipient of Abel Prize and Knuth Prize, representing Europe (Hungary).

Virginia Vassilevska Williams.topics: Algorithm design, matrix multiplication, fine-grained complexity, graph algorithms, dynamic algorithms
Virginia Vassilevska Williams.key_contributions: A series of results that improved the exponent of fast matrix multiplication, established fine-grained complexity (conditional lower bounds based on SETH, etc.), and had a major impact on shortest path/dynamic graph algorithms.
Virginia Vassilevska Williams.inclusion_reason: Co-founder of fine-grained complexity and a leading expert on matrix multiplication research. A female researcher from Europe who contributes to diversity.

Ryan Williams.topics: Computational complexity, circuit lower bound, fine-grained complexity, algorithm design, SAT
Ryan Williams.key_contributions: Contributed to the development of fine-grained complexity by proving the circuit lower bound NEXP⊄ACC0 using an algorithmic method (a program that derives the lower bound from an algorithm).
Ryan Williams.inclusion_reason: A top active complexity theorist who has established an innovative method to connect the circuit lower bound and algorithms.

Jelani Nelson.topics: Streaming algorithms, sketching, dimensionality reduction, randomized linear algebra, computational statistics
Jelani Nelson.key_contributions: Contributed to the basic theory of big data processing with the optimality proof of Johnson-Lindenstrauss dimension reduction, Sparse JL transform, and sketching/streaming algorithms.
Jelani Nelson.inclusion_reason: A leading expert on streaming and dimensionality reduction theory. Great contribution to diversity, including contribution to education (AddisCoder).

Ola Svensson.topics: Approximation algorithms, combinatorial optimization, traveling salesman problem, scheduling, network design
Ola Svensson.key_contributions: Won multiple best paper awards for constant multiplication approximation of the asymmetric traveling salesman problem (ATSP) (solving a long-standing unsolved problem), symmetric TSP, and scheduling approximation algorithms.
Ola Svensson.inclusion_reason: Current leader in approximation algorithms (especially TSP). A young mid-career who represents Europe.

Éva Tardos.topics: Combinatorial optimization, network flow, algorithmic game theory, price anarchy (PoA), approximation algorithm
Éva Tardos.key_contributions: Fundamental contributions in strong polynomial time network flow/minimum cost flow algorithms, establishment of algorithmic game theory (analysis of price disorder), and approximation algorithms.
Éva Tardos.inclusion_reason: A leading figure in combinatorial optimization and algorithmic game theory. A female researcher from Hungary who contributes to diversity.

Alexander A. Razborov.topics: Computational complexity, circuit lower bound, proof complexity, communication complexity, combinatorics
Alexander A. Razborov.key_contributions: Fundamental contributions in hyperpolynomial lower bounds of monotone circuits, wall theory of natural proofs (co-authored with Rudich), proof complexity and communication complexity.
Alexander A. Razborov.inclusion_reason: An important figure in circuit lower bounds and proof complexity. Recipient of the Nevanlinna Prize, a native of Russia who contributes to diversity.

Omer Reingold.topics: Computational complexity, pseudorandom numbers, expanded graphs, cryptography, algorithmic fairness
Omer Reingold.key_contributions: Contributed to the proof that undirected graph connectivity can be solved in the log domain (SL=L), expanded graph construction using zig-zag products, and pseudorandom numbers and algorithmic fairness.
Omer Reingold.inclusion_reason: A stalwart known for his important results (SL=L) on domain complexity and pseudo-random numbers. Gödel Prize winner, from Israel.

David Zuckerman.topics: Computational complexity, randomness extractor, pseudorandom numbers, derandomization, coding theory
David Zuckerman.key_contributions: Developed the theory of randomness extractor and created an innovative configuration of two-source extractor/resilient function with Chattopadhyay. Contributed to the theory of pseudorandom numbers and hardness.
David Zuckerman.inclusion_reason: A leading expert on randomness extractor theory. Winner of the 2025 Gödel Prize.

Eshan Chattopadhyay.topics: Randomness extractor, pseudorandom numbers, computational complexity, derandomization
Eshan Chattopadhyay.key_contributions: Co-authored with Zuckerman to construct an explicit two-source extractor and a resilient function, solving a long-standing open problem. Great progress has been made in randomness extraction theory.
Eshan Chattopadhyay.inclusion_reason: A young man who won the 2025 Gödel Prize for his breakthrough work on a two-source extractor. Being from India contributes to diversity.

Ankur Moitra.topics: Algorithmic machine learning, robust statistics, tensor decomposition, optimization, computational complexity
Ankur Moitra.key_contributions: He is a leader in rigorous algorithm theory for machine learning, including efficient algorithms for high-dimensional robust statistics, learning of Gaussian mixture distributions, non-negative matrix factorization and tensor methods.
Ankur Moitra.inclusion_reason: A leading expert on the boundaries between learning theory and algorithms. Winner of the 2026 Gödel Prize, who is of Indian descent and contributes to diversity.

Ilias Diakonikolas.topics: Algorithmic high-dimensional statistics, robust learning, distribution learning, optimization, trade-offs between computation and statistics
Ilias Diakonikolas.key_contributions: Leading research on the development of efficient algorithms for high-dimensional robust estimation, optimal sample complexity for distribution learning, and the gap between computation and statistics (SQ lower bound).
Ilias Diakonikolas.inclusion_reason: A central figure in robust statistics and algorithmic learning theory. 2026 Gödel Prize winner, from Greece.

Kazuo Iwama.topics: Algorithm, stable matching, quantum circuit, SAT, graph algorithm
Kazuo Iwama.key_contributions: Research on approximations and variants of stable marriage problems, complexity of quantum circuits, and SAT algorithms. He contributed to the creation of the Asian Algorithm and Computing Community (AAAC).
Kazuo Iwama.inclusion_reason: Japan's leading TCS researcher. A central figure in building the theoretical computer science community in Asia.

Osamu Watanabe.topics: Computational complexity, analysis of algorithms, randomization algorithms, average case complexity, SAT
Osamu Watanabe.key_contributions: Research on computational complexity (completeness/reduction), randomization algorithms and phase transitions, and average case analysis of SAT. He has led the Japanese TCS research community for many years.
Osamu Watanabe.inclusion_reason: A leading figure in computational complexity research in Japan. Contributing to the diversity of the Asian region.

Takeshi Tokuyama.topics: Computational geometry, combinatorial optimization, algorithms, discrete geometry, data structures
Takeshi Tokuyama.key_contributions: Research on computational geometry and combinatorial optimization algorithms, k-level complexity, and geometric optimization. Leading the Japanese computational geometry/discrete algorithm community.
Takeshi Tokuyama.inclusion_reason: Japan's leading computational geometry/algorithm researcher. A key figure contributing to Asia's diversity.

Pinyan Lu.topics: Algorithmic game theory, computational complexity, mechanism design, approximation algorithms, optimization
Pinyan Lu.key_contributions: Research on dichotomy and holant problems, algorithmic game theory and mechanism design. Leading TCS research in China.
Pinyan Lu.inclusion_reason: China's leading researcher in computational complexity and algorithmic game theory. Contributing to Asia's diversity.

Xiaotie Deng.topics: Algorithmic game theory, equilibrium calculation, online algorithm, mechanism design, combinatorial optimization
Xiaotie Deng.key_contributions: Complexity of Nash equilibrium/market equilibrium calculations, fundamentals of algorithmic game theory, research on online algorithms. Leading algorithm and game theory research in China.
Xiaotie Deng.inclusion_reason: China's leading figure in algorithmic game theory and equilibrium calculations. Contributing to Asia's diversity.

Manindra Agrawal.topics: Computational complexity, primality testing, algebraic complexity, derandomization, computational number theory
Manindra Agrawal.key_contributions: Discovered the AKS primality test method (testing primality in deterministic polynomial time; co-authored with Kayal and Saxena), which had a great impact on computational number theory and complexity. He is also a leading figure in research on algebraic complexity.
Manindra Agrawal.inclusion_reason: Received the Gödel Prize and Fulkerson Prize for the AKS primality test method. He is one of India's leading TCS researchers and contributes to diversity.

Venkatesan Guruswami.topics: Coding theory, list decoding, computational complexity, difficulty of approximation, pseudorandom numbers
Venkatesan Guruswami.key_contributions: Fundamental contributions in coding theory and TCS, including list decoding of algebraic-geometric codes with Sudan, codes reaching capacity, and the bisection theorem for approximation difficulty/constraint satisfaction problems (CSP).
Venkatesan Guruswami.inclusion_reason: A leading expert on list decoding and coding theory. Winner of the Gödel Prize and Presburger Prize, born in India and contributing to diversity.

Nisheeth K. Vishnoi.topics: Continuous optimization, algorithms, sampling, algorithmic fairness, machine learning theory
Nisheeth K. Vishnoi.key_contributions: Research on continuous optimization and algorithms (interior point method/gradient method theory), sampling/volume calculation algorithms, and algorithmic fairness. Bridging optimization and AI theory.
Nisheeth K. Vishnoi.inclusion_reason: A leading expert on the boundaries between continuous optimization and algorithms/AI theory. Being from India contributes to diversity.

Anna R. Karlin.topics: Online algorithms, randomization algorithms, algorithmic game theory, approximation algorithms, TSP
Anna R. Karlin.key_contributions: Fundamentals of online algorithms and competitive analysis, randomization algorithms, and recent breakthroughs in improving the approximation ratio of symmetric TSP to less than 3/2 (co-authored with Klein and Oveis Gharan).
Anna R. Karlin.inclusion_reason: Known for the fundamentals of online algorithms and the latest breakthroughs in TSP approximation. Female researchers contribute to diversity.

Tim Roughgarden.topics: Algorithmic game theory, price disorder, mechanism design, approximation algorithm, blockchain theory
Tim Roughgarden.key_contributions: He is a leading figure in the analysis of price anarchy, its robustness, mechanism design, and the theory of blockchain transaction fee mechanisms in recent years.
Tim Roughgarden.inclusion_reason: A leading expert on algorithmic game theory (price disorder). Received the Gödel Prize.

Scott Aaronson.topics: Quantum complexity, quantum complexity, quantum supremacy, computational complexity, theory of AI safety
Scott Aaronson.key_contributions: He is a leader in quantum complexity through his research on quantum complexity theory (BQP and polynomial hierarchies, Boson Sampling, and the theoretical foundations of quantum supremacy), quantum testimony, and the learnability of quantum states.
Scott Aaronson.inclusion_reason: An active leader in quantum complexity theory. Received the ACM Prize in Computing.

Ran Raz.topics: Computational complexity, communication complexity, proof complexity, quantum complexity, circuit lower bound
Ran Raz.key_contributions: Fundamental contributions in the parallel repetition theorem, lower bounds on communication complexity and branching programs, trade-offs between memory and learning, and lower bounds on quantum information.
Ran Raz.inclusion_reason: A leading figure in communication complexity and proof complexity. Fundamental results such as parallel iteration theorem. Originally from Israel, contributing to diversity.

Irit Dinur.topics: Computational complexity, PCP, difficulty of approximation, coding theory, high-dimensional expansion
Irit Dinur.key_contributions: The combinatorial concise proof of the PCP theorem by gap amplification has had a great impact in recent years, with research on high-dimensional expanded graphs leading to locally testable codes (c³-LTC) and quantum LDPC codes.
Irit Dinur.inclusion_reason: A leading expert on PCP theory and codes/expansion graphs. Gödel Prize winner, female, Israeli-born, and contributing to diversity.

Aleksander Mądry.topics: Fast graph algorithms, maximum flow, continuous optimization, machine learning robustness, optimization
Aleksander Mądry.key_contributions: High-speed algorithm of maximum flow/minimum cost flow (groundbreaking speedup using electric flow/interior point method), and in recent years has led the theory of robustness and adversarial examples of machine learning models.
Aleksander Mądry.inclusion_reason: A leading expert on the boundaries between fast graph algorithms and robust machine learning. Born in Poland and contributes to diversity.

Luca Trevisan.topics: Computational complexity, pseudorandom numbers, approximation algorithms, spectral graph theory, randomness extractor
Luca Trevisan.key_contributions: Fundamental contributions in pseudo-random number generator (Trevisan extractor) from hardness, approximation difficulty, spectral graph theory and graph partitioning algorithm.
Luca Trevisan.inclusion_reason: A stalwart of pseudorandom numbers and spectral algorithms. Representing Europe (Italy). Note: Although he passed away in 2024, he was an important figure who formed the foundation of the field.

Kevin Leyton-Brown.topics: Algorithmic game theory, auction/market design, mechanism design, empirical algorithm analysis
Kevin Leyton-Brown.key_contributions: Wrote the standard textbook on multi-agent systems, ``Multiagent Systems'' (co-authored with Shoham). He is known for his calculation methods for combinatorial auctions, frequency band reorganization (FCC incentive auction), and portfolio-based algorithm selection (SATzilla).
Kevin Leyton-Brown.inclusion_reason: A central figure in algorithmic game theory and market design, and the author of a representative textbook in the multi-agent field.

Sarit Kraus.topics: Automatic negotiation, cooperation and coalition formation, information agents, human-agent interaction
Sarit Kraus.key_contributions: Opened up the research field of automatic negotiation between self-interested agents and built a formal model and actual system for coalition formation and team formation. Author of "Strategic Negotiation in Multiagent Environments".
Sarit Kraus.inclusion_reason: He is the founder of the field of automatic negotiation and a leading expert in the field of multi-agents who won the IJCAI Computers and Thought award.

Jeffrey S. Rosenschein.topics: Automatic negotiation, computational social choice, voting theory, mechanism design
Jeffrey S. Rosenschein.key_contributions: Introducing the basics of economics and game theory to automatic negotiation between computers. His book ``Rules of Encounter'' (1994) with Gilad Zlotkin is a classic of multi-agent negotiation.
Jeffrey S. Rosenschein.inclusion_reason: A pioneer in the economic foundation of multi-agent systems and an IFAAMAS Influential Paper winner.

Edith Elkind.topics: Computational social selection, fair division, algorithmic game theory, federated games
Edith Elkind.key_contributions: Numerous fundamental results in computational social choice and cooperative game theory. Researches the computational complexity of voting protocols, coalition structure formation, and election control and manipulation.
Edith Elkind.inclusion_reason: A leading researcher in the field of computational social selection and a SIGAI Autonomous Agents Research Award recipient. Diversity of female researchers in Europe.

Ariel D. Procaccia.topics: Computational social choice, fair division, algorithmic economics, democratic decision making
Ariel D. Procaccia.key_contributions: Fundamental contribution in a computational protocol for envy-free allocation. Fair division tool Spliddit, practical application of participatory budgeting and election methods.
Ariel D. Procaccia.inclusion_reason: A leading expert on computational social choice and fair division, and an AAAI/ACM Fellow who won the Computers and Thought Award.

Michael L. Littman.topics: Reinforcement learning, Markov games, decision making under uncertainty, multi-agent learning
Michael L. Littman.key_contributions: Markov game formulation as a framework for multi-agent reinforcement learning (1994), basic research on POMDP/sequential decision-making algorithm.
Michael L. Littman.inclusion_reason: Author of the founding paper on multi-agent reinforcement learning (Markov games) and AAAI/ACM Fellow.

Peter Stone.topics: Multi-agent learning, reinforcement learning agent, RoboCup, ad hoc teamwork
